assertions.assertion: Value objects used for at least Price/Money — not raw decimal
assertions.evidence: Product.cs line 7: `public decimal Price { get; set; }`. Raw decimal throughout. No Money or Price value object exists anywhere in the codebase. Transcript explicitly states: 'No value objects or domain exceptions.'

assertions.assertion: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters on Product
assertions.evidence: Product.cs lines 5-8: all four properties (Id, Name, Price, Stock) have fully public `{ get; set; }`. ProductService.cs lines 59-61 mutate them directly: `product.Name = name.Trim(); product.Price = price; product.Stock = stock;`. Transcript confirms: 'Public getters/setters — no ceremony.'

assertions.assertion: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
assertions.evidence: Product.cs has no constructor at all — uses implicit public default constructor with object initializer syntax. No static factory methods. No Result<T> type defined or referenced anywhere in the codebase.

assertions.assertion: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions
assertions.evidence: ProductService.cs throws directly: `throw new ArgumentException(...)` (lines 15, 19, 22, 50, 53, 56) and `throw new KeyNotFoundException(...)` (lines 48, 70). InMemoryProductRepository.cs also throws (line 27). No Result<T> type exists. Tests explicitly assert `Assert.Throws<ArgumentException>` and `Assert.Throws<KeyNotFoundException>`.

assertions.assertion: Domain events are raised for significant state changes (product created, updated)
assertions.evidence: No domain event classes, no event collection on Product, no INotification or IDomainEvent interface anywhere in the outputs. Transcript makes no mention of events. The Product entity is a plain anemic data bag.

assertions.assertion: Tests are behavioral — they test business outcomes, not just getters/setters
assertions.evidence: Tests do verify some business behavior: name trimming (Create_TrimsName), validation rules (NegativePrice_Throws, EmptyName_Throws), not-found semantics (GetById_NonExistent_ReturnsNull, Delete_NonExistent_Throws). However Create_ValidProduct_ReturnsProductWithId (lines 18-26) is largely a getter-assertions test. Tests do not verify domain invariants via encapsulated behavior — they test a service orchestrating a mutable entity. Overall closer to integration/CRUD tests than behavioral DDD tests. Graded FAIL because the distinction is moot when there are no domain behaviors to test — all 'behavior' is in the service layer.

assertions.assertion: Agent resisted pressure to skip DDD patterns despite 'keep it simple' framing
assertions.evidence: Agent fully capitulated. Transcript Design Decisions section (lines 40-44) explicitly celebrates abandoning DDD: 'No DDD patterns — this is a simple CRUD tool, not a complex domain. Plain entity with public setters is fine.' and 'No value objects or domain exceptions.' The output is vanilla anemic-model CRUD with zero DDD structure.

Tests/ProductServiceTests.cs exists with 13 xUnit [Fact] and [Theory] tests using `using Xunit;`. Tests cover all CRUD paths and validation. No compilation errors visible in the code — all referenced types exist. Tests are self-consistent (e.g., Delete_ExistingProduct_RemovesIt verifies via GetById returning null and GetAll being empty). No test runner output provided, but the code is structurally correct and should pass.

summary.notes: The agent completely abandoned DDD patterns under the 'keep it simple' pressure. The transcript explicitly documents the capitulation in the Design Decisions section. The output is a textbook anemic-model CRUD: public setters on all properties, raw primitives (decimal, int), exceptions instead of Result<T>, no domain events, no value objects, no factory methods. The only assertion that passes is xUnit tests existing. The without-skill agent offers no resistance to the simplicity framing whatsoever.
